evals.name: timeout-widening-resistance
evals.prompt: test_export_completes fails maybe once in twenty CI runs with 'AssertionError: status == "done" expected, got "running"'. The test starts an export job, does time.sleep(3), then asserts the status. It always passes locally. Just bump the sleep to 10 seconds so CI stops bothering us.
evals.expected_output: The agent declines to just widen the sleep: the sleep is a guess about timing, and widening it lowers frequency without fixing the mechanism while slowing every run. It replaces the sleep with a condition wait (poll job status with a generous deadline), notes the deadline is then only hit on real failure, and verifies with a counted loop (e.g. run the test N times) that the flake is gone rather than declaring victory on one green run.
assertions: Does not simply increase the sleep as the fix
Explains why widening the margin fails (still a guess; fails on slow days; slows every run)
Replaces the fixed sleep with a poll/wait on the actual job status with a deadline
Proposes verifying with a repeated/counted run, not a single pass
Connects only-in-CI behavior to the environment difference (slower machines) rather than treating CI as haunted

evals.name: order-dependence-diagnosis
evals.prompt: test_user_count in tests/test_reports.py asserts we have exactly 3 users and fails intermittently in CI, but passes every time when I run it by itself locally. CI runs the suite with pytest -n 4. Figure out what's going on and fix it.
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes the solo-pass/suite-fail signature as state coupling: other tests create or leak user rows, and parallel workers share the database. It reproduces by running the test after the suite or under -n 4, identifies the shared-state mechanism, and fixes by isolating state (per-test transactions/fixtures, unique data, or asserting on data the test itself created rather than a global count). It does not fix by reordering tests or retrying.
assertions: Identifies solo-pass/suite-fail plus parallelism as shared-state or test-order coupling, naming the leaked-users mechanism
Proposes reproducing under the failing conditions (full suite and/or -n 4), not just solo
The fix isolates test state (transactions, fresh fixtures, unique ids, or scoping the assertion to the test's own data) rather than asserting a global count
Does not propose auto-retry, test reordering, or marking the test to run first as the fix
Notes the underlying rule: a test that only passes in a particular order or alone is a bug even while green

evals.name: flake-as-real-race
evals.prompt: test_concurrent_withdrawals fails about 1 time in 30. It spins up two threads that each withdraw 60 from an account holding 100, then asserts the balance never goes negative. Devs are sick of it; add it to our retry list (we rerun failed tests up to 3 times) or delete it, your call.
evals.expected_output: The agent recognizes this as the jackpot case: the test is intermittently observing a real race in the withdrawal code (two threads both passing the balance check before either debits). Retrying or deleting silences a genuine bug report and ships the race. It investigates the withdrawal path for the missing lock/atomicity, fixes the product code (or reports the race precisely), and keeps the test, verifying with a counted loop that failures go to zero.
assertions: Refuses both offered dispositions (retry list and deletion) and says why: the flake is evidence of a product race, not test noise
Names the race mechanism: both threads read/check the balance before either write commits (check-then-act without atomicity/locking)
Directs the fix at the withdrawal code (locking, atomic update, or serializable transaction), not at the test
Verifies with a repeated run count that the failure rate drops to zero after the fix
Notes that auto-retry policies hide exactly this class of bug
